Facts.text: Syrian harbor city Saida (Sidon) surrenders to Crusaders
Adrian IV elected Pope. The only Englishman to become pontiff, Nicholas Breakspear was a member of the family which until recent years brewed beer in Henley-on-Thames, Oxfordshire.
Crusaders wound Rabbi Elezar ben Judah
Battle of Baza - Spanish army captures Baza from Moors
Council of Trent holds its last session after 18 years, the last ecumenical council for more than 300 years
First European peace congress opens in Munster
Middelburg, Netherlands forbids building of synagogue
Father Marquette builds first dwelling in what is now Chicago
Battle of Lund: A Danish army under the command of King Christian V of Denmark engages the Swedish army commanded by Field Marshal Simon Grundel-Helmfelt
Hen in Rome lays a uniquely patterned egg, later believed to have predicted the arrival of the Kirch/Newton "Great Comet of 1680"
First General Assembly in Pennsylvania (Chester)
Emperor Leopold I takes control of Transylvania
Spanish king Carlos II names Maximilian II as Viceroy of Southern Netherlands
Bonnie Prince Charles reaches Derby
General Washington bids officers farewell at Fraunce's Tavern, NYC
Rebellious Flemish farmers occupy Hasselt
Peter Gaillard of Lancaster, Pennsylvania, patents a horse-drawn mower
Britain outlaws "suttee" in India, a Hindu practice where a widow burns herself to death on her husband's funeral pyre
French army begins bombing citadel of Antwerp
American Anti-Slavery Society formed by Arthur Tappan in Philadelphia
Whig party holds its first national convention, Harrisburg, Pennsylvania
Manila paper (made from sails, canvas and rope) patented in Massachusetts
President Louis Napoleon Boaparte's forces crush an attempted coup d'etat in France
Battle of Waynesborough (Brier Creek), Georgia
Romanian Jews are forbidden to practise law
Former Minnesota farmer Oliver Hudson Kelley founds the Order of the Patrons of Husbandry (better known today as the Grange)
The first edition of the Los Angeles Times newspaper is published
Explorer Henery Morton Stanley's expedition reaches Bagamoyo in Indian Ocean
56th Congress (1899-1901) convenes
Webb Hayes, son of US President Rutherford Hayes, receives medal of honor
Anne Russell's play "'Girl and the judge" premieres in New York
Alpha Phi Alpha, first Black Greek Letter Fraternity, forms
Haiti's president General Alexis Nord flees from military coup
The world's ten leading maritime nations attend a Naval Conference in London; they agree on rules for blockade, convoys, and seizure of contraband
Oldest still-operating NHL franchise is officially established as J. Ambrose O’Brien and Jack Laviolette create the “Club de Hockey Canadien,” known today as the Montreal Canadiens
Frank Friday Fletcher is first US admiral to receive Congressional Medal of Honor
Ku Klux Klan receives charter from Fulton County, Georgia
Panama-Pacific International Exposition closes in San Francisco
1st Pro football playoff game Buffalo-7, Canton-3 at Polo Grounds, NYC
American Professional Football Association Championship, Cubs Park, Chicago: Chicago Staleys beat Buffalo All-Americans, 10-7 in de facto championship game after teams tied in standings
The Virginia Rappe manslaughter trial against Roscoe 'Fatty' Arbuckle ends in a hung jury
Lucille Atcherson, becomes first woman legation sect-US foreign service
WEAF radio begins broadcasting Eveready Hour (variety show)
Pittsburgh Pirates future Baseball Hall of Fame outfielder Paul Waner wins NL MVP
Walter Donaldson & Gus Kahn's musical "Whoopee!", starring Eddie Cantor, premieres on Broadway at the New Amsterdam Theatre, NYC; runs for 407 performances
Vatican approves rhythm method for birth control
Jack Kirkland's play "Tobacco Road" premieres in NYC, became the longest-running play of its time
1,200 at St Joseph's College (Philadelphia) enroll in anticommunism class
Nazi ordinance places Jews of Poland outside protection of courts
FDRorders dismantling of Works Progress Administration
First US citizenship granted to an alien on foreign soil (James Hoey)
Holocaust: In Warsaw, Zofia Kossak-Szczucka and Wanda Filipowicz set up the Żegota organization
US bombers strike Italian mainland for first time in WW II
Second Cairo Conference:FDR, Winston Churchill and Turkish President İsmet İnönü meet (till Dec 6)
Yugoslavian resistance forms provisionary government under Dr Ribar
Germans destroy Rhine dikes, Betuwe flooded
11th Heisman Trophy Award: Doc Blanchard, Army (FB)
Senate approves US participation in United Nations
USSR joins International Amateur Athletic Union
SS Kiangya hits mine in Whangpoo River, China, sinks killing 2,750
Bob Gage ties NFL record of a 97 yard touchdown run

Duncan Stewart, 2nd British Governor of Sarawak is fatally stabbed in the streets of Sibu by Malay student Rosli Dhoby with help from Morshidi Sidek with the goal of helping neighbouring Indonesia to take over British Sarawak
Mir Waiz Maulvi Muhammad Yusouf is appointed President of Azad Kashmir Government
Superheated gasses roll down Mount Catarman, Philippines, killing 500 people
Walter P. Reuther chosen chairman of the Congress of Industrial Organizations (CIO) in the US
The first Burger King fast-food restaurant opens in Miami, Florida
22nd Heisman Trophy Award:Paul Hornung, Notre Dame (QB)
American diver Pat McCormick wins the 3m springboard gold medal at the Melbourne Olympics with 142.46 points; later wraps up the women's double when she takes the 10m platform gold
Australian swimmer Murray Rose sets world record 4:27.3 to claim the 400m gold medal at the Melbourne Olympics; Rose later also wins 1,500m gold
2 commuter trains collide in heavy fog killing 92 (St John's, England)
First edition of "Chase's Annual Events" published

American pilots Bob Timm and John Cook take off from Las Vegas, Nevada in "The Hacienda", their modified Cessna 172, in an attempt to break recently set airplane flight endurance record; effort succeeds almost 65 days later
Dahomey (Benin), Ivory Coast become autonomous within French Community
Finnish government of Fagerholm resigns
Smallest NY Knick, 49th St Madison Square Garden crowd-1,300 (snowstorm)
Tanganyika becomes 104th member of UN
WXGA TV channel 8 in Waycross, GA (PBS) begins broadcasting
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Boston Bruins' right wing Andy Hebenton sets a new NHL record by playing in his 581st consecutive game in 2-2 tie with Chicago Black Hawks; reaches 630 straight games
PopePaul VIcloses second session of 2nd Vatican Council
Baseball approves a free-agent draft
Commissioner's office given full powers in baseball disputes
Test Cricket debut of Ian Chappell, v Pakistan MCG, 11, 0-49, 0-31
The Beatles release "Beatles For Sale", their fourth studio album in the UK
2 passenger planes collide above Danbury, Connecticut, 4 die
2nd NY Knicks game postponed (due to death of opponent 76ers' owner)
KETS TV channel 2 in Little Rock, AR (PBS) begins broadcasting
Military Working Dog "Nemo" saves the life of his handler Airman Robert A. Throneburg during the Vietnam War, surviving a gunshot wound to the nose
Following a civil rights march in Dungannon, Northern Ireland, there is a violent clash between Loyalists and those who are taking part in the march
Unemployment in US increases to 5.8%

McGurk's Bar bombing: the UVF explode a bomb at a Catholic-owned pub in Belfast, killing fifteen Catholic civilians and wounding seventeen others; this was the highest death toll from a single incident in Belfast during 'the Troubles'
The Indian Navy attacks the Pakistan Navy and Karachi
The UN Security Council calls an emergency session to consider the deteriorating situation between India and Pakistan
Dutch DC-8 charter crashes in Sri Lanka killing 191 Muslim pilgrims
6 South Molukkans occupy Indonesian consulate in The Hague, 1 dead
Neil Simon's play "Chapter Two" opens in NYC
NFL's 5,000th game, Cincinnati beats KC 27-7
Dianne Feinstein is named as San Francisco's 1st female and Jewish mayor after the assassination of George Moscone, then elected for two full terms
Pioneer Venus 1 goes into orbit around Venus
Cleveland Cavaliers retire jersey # 7, Bingo Smith
Islanders end 15 game undefeated streak (13-0-2) (Col Rockies)
"Falcon Crest" premieres on CBS-TV
According to South Africa, Ciskei gains independence. Not recognized as an independent country outside South Africa.
48th Heisman Trophy Award: Herschel Walker, Georgia (RB)
China adopts its constitution
Police and racist demonstrators clash in Antwerp
NJ Devils 1st shut-out, beating Minnesota North Stars 6-0
US jet fighters strike Syrian anti-aircraft positions in Lebanon
Hezbollah militants hijack a Kuwait Airlines plane, killing four passengers.
President Reaganappoints Vice Admiral John Poindexter as security adviser
US launches its Fleet Satellite Communications System (Fltsatcom-7)
Karlstad skates world record 10 km (13:48.51)
Actor Gary Busey critically injured in motorcycle crash
Edmonton center Jimmy Carson becomes just the third player to score 6 NHL hat tricks before the age of 21 in the Oilers 10-6 win over the New York Rangers
USSR performs nuclear test at Novaya Zemlya USSR
NBC's premiere of "Howard Beach: Making A Case for Murder", based on December 1986 murders of black youths by white youths in New York City

An Iraqi official reports that Iraq will withdraw if it can retain control of the Rumailah field and keep Bubiyan and Werbah islands; also says that demands that the Palestinian issue be treated separately would not be surmountable
Due to Persian Gulf crisis gas hits $1.60 per gallon price in NYC
Iraq announces it will release all 3,300 Soviet hostages
Country music mother and daughter act The Judds "farewell" concert at Murphy Center in Murfreesboro, Tennessee; later re-unions and "farewells" in 2000, 2010, 2015, and 2021
Muslim Shi'ites release last US hostage, journalist Terry Anderson, after 6½ years of captivity
Pan American World Airways ceased operations
Patricia Bowman testifies that William Kennedy Smith raped her
A truce is concluded between the government of Angola and UNITA rebels.
Johann Koss skates world record 5K (6:35.53)
Atherton (185*) bats for 643 minutes to save Johannesburg Test
NASA's 1st Mars rover launched from Cape Canaveral
Orlando Magic tie NBA record of fewest points scored since inception of 24 second clock losing to Cleveland Cavalier, 84-57
"Diary of Anne Frank" opens at Music Box Theater NYC
NBA suspends former Golden State Warriors guard Latrell Sprewell for 1 year for attacking coach P.J. Carlesimo
Nizar Hamdoon warns that Iraq will not allow oil to flow during a third six-month phase of the UN's oil-for-food sale until the UN approves an aid distribution plan
The Unity Module, the second module of the International Space Station, is launched
Tens of thousands of people in Hong Kong protest for democracy and call on the Government to allow universal and equal suffrage
An adult giant squid is caught on video by Kubodera near the Ogasawara Islands, 1,000 km (620 miles) south of Tokyo
Montreal Canadiens retire Hall of Fame center Elmer Lach's jersey (No. 16), as part of team's centennial celebration
29 people are killed by a mortar attack in Bteeha, Syria
Typhoon Bopha makes landfall in the Philippines, killing at least 81 people
Mosconi Cup nine-ball pool, Las Vegas, Nevada: 4 consecutive titles for Europe after an 11-2 thrashing of US; MVP: Niels Feijen (Netherlands)
Xavier Bettel becomes Luxenberg's first openly gay Prime Minister
Mosconi Cup nine-ball pool, Blackpool, England: Team Europe beats US, 11-5 for 5th straight title; MVP: Niels Feijen (Netherlands)
The United Nations warns that the world is on course for the warmest year since records began
Ukraine and Pro-Russian rebels agree to cease fire in the eastern war zone, beginning December 9
US authorities promise a "fair" investigation into the death of African American Eric Garner, after a white New York City police officer held him in a choke-hold faces no charges
Floods in Chennai and Tamil Nadu state, India start receding after a month of heavy rainfall, leaving more 260 dead and thousands stranded
Austria elects liberal independent Alexander Van der Bellen as President, after original vote in May annulled
Tens of thousands march throughout Brazil against a vote to undermine anti-corruption investigations
Venezuela issues new higher-value notes after currency falls 60% in 1 months against the US dollar
New York Giants fire head coach Ben McAdoo and manager Jerry Reese after a 2–10 start
New York's Metropolitan Opera suspends conductor James Levine after allegations of sexual misconduct
Thomas fire begins and spreads to city of Ventura, California
US Supreme Court allowsPresident Trump's travel ban to come into effect for 6 mostly Muslim countries
Attempts to establish mob council in Sicily thwarted when 46 members of the mafia arrested including Settimio Mineo
First successful birth resulting from uterus transplant from a deceased donor in São Paulo, Brazil
French couture house Chanel ends its use of fur and exotic skins following bans by other companies
Ownership group Seattle Hockey Partners led by billionaire businessman Davis Bonderman is unanimously granted an NHL license by the Board of Governors; to start play 2020-21 season at KeyArena
Research published in "Nature" and "Science" claims Native Americans had just one migration from Siberia to the New World, at most 23,000 years ago
Scottish artist Charlotte Prodger wins the 2018 Turner Prize with film shot on her iPhone
58 migrants drown after a boat sinks off the coast of Mauritania, with 83 saved
North American migratory birds getting smaller, their wings wider due to climate change according to study by University of Michigan published in journal "Ecology Letters"
Mosconi Cup nine-ball pool, Coventry, England: Europe ends US 2-year run with emphatic 11-3 win; MVP: Jayson Shaw (Scotland)
New Zealand cricket captain Kane Williamson smashes 251 to set up the home side's innings & 134 run win over the West Indies in the 1st Test in Hamilton
Mt Semeru erupts on Java island, Indonesia, killing at least 14 and injuring 56
New Zealand cricket spin bowler Ajaz Patel (10-119) joins Jim Laker and Anil Kumble as the only three men to take all ten wickets in a Test innings, on Day 2 of the 2nd Test against India in Mumbai
At least 27 people killed after a bus is buried in a landslide in Risaralda province, western Colombia
Mt Semeru, Indonesia’s highest volcano, erupts releasing gas clouds and rivers of lava on the island of Java
French government led by Prime Minister Michel Barnier is toppled by a no confidence vote for the first time since 1962
United Healthcare CEO Brian Thompson is shot and killed outside a hotel in Manhattan, New York, in a targeted attack
Treaty of Paris: English KingHenry IIIand French KingLouis IXend 100 years of conflict between the Capetian and Plantagenet dynasties
Ottoman SultanSuleiman the Magnificentoccupies Baghdad
Jean Racine's tragedy "Alexandre le Grand" premieres at the Palais Royal Theater in Paris
GeneralJohn Churchill(later 1st Duke of Marlborough) changes allegiance fromJames IIto William of Orange
James Monroeis elected to become the 5th President of the United States, defeating Rufus King of the Federalist Party
Robert Schumann's oratorio "Das Paradies und die Peri" premieres in Leipzig
James Knox Polkelected 11th US President
Influential New York politician William MagearTweed"Boss Tweed" of Tammany Hall escapes from jail where he was being held for embezzlement (flees to Spain but later recaptured)
British Prime MinisterArthur Balfourresigns after three years in office
Walter Johnsonaccepts an advance from the Chicago Whales of the Federal League; Washington Senators ownerClark Griffiththreatens to take Johnson to court
Henry Ford's peace ship, Oscar II, sails for Europe 'to get the boys out of the trenches by Christmas'
US PresidentWoodrow Wilsonsails for Versailles Peace Conference in France
Dmitri Shostakovich's 2nd Symphony premieres in Moscow
Duke Ellingtonopens at the Cotton Club in Harlem, NYC
Kurt von Schleicher succeedsFranz von Papenas Reich Chancellor of Germany
FDRcreates Federal Alcohol Control Administration
MLB CommissionerKenesaw Mountain Landisannounces that any baseball club may sign Negro players
Aaron Copland's andJerome Robbins' ballet "The Pied Piper" premieres in New York City
An impromptu recording session by the "Million-Dollar Quartet" -Elvis Presley,Carl Perkins,Jerry Lee Lewis, andJohnny Cash- takes place at Sun Studios in Memphis, Tennessee
1962 NFL Draft:Ernie Davis, University of Syracuse RB #1 pick by Washington Redskins
Floyd PattersonKOs Tom McNeeley in 4 for heavyweight boxing title

NY's Museum of Modern Art hangsHenri Matisse's picture "Le Bateau" the right side up, after stockbroker Genevieve Habert notices it is displayed upside down, the first of 116,000 people in 47 days to notice
The female contraceptive 'pill' becomes available on the National Health Service in Britain
Aldo Moroforms Italian government (1963-1968)
NASA launches Gemini 7 withFrank BormanandJim Lovell- later the focus of the first crewed space rendezvous
San Francisco GiantMasanori Murakami, 4-1 this year, does not renew his contract signing instead with the Nankai Hawks of Osaka for $40,000

Montreux Casino, in Montreux, Switzerland, burns down to the ground after a fan fires a flare gun during concert byFrank Zappaand The Mothers of Invention; incident inspires rock band Deep Purple's song "Smoke on the Water"
Jean-Paul Sartrevisits Red Army Faction (Baader–Meinhof Gang) leaderAndreas Baaderin prison
Dutch war criminalPieter Mentenfreed
Two months after death of drummerJohn Bonham, Led Zeppelin announces they will disband
ReaganExecutive Order on Intelligence (No 12333) that allows CIA to engage in domestic counter-intelligence
French PresidentFrançois Mitterrandreceives Polish leaderWojciech Jaruzelski

Defending championIvan Lendlof Czechoslovakia wins 3rd straight season-ending ATP Masters Grand Prix tennis title by defeatingMats Wilanderof Sweden 6–2, 6–2, 6–3 in the final at Madison Square Garden, NYC
Orioles trade veteran 1BEddie Murrayto the Dodgers
US PresidentGeorge H. W. Bushorders 28,000 US troops to war-torn Somalia
Dan Jansenskates world record 500m (35.92 sec)

Kennedy Center Honors ceremony held for actorKirk Douglas, singerAretha Franklin, composerMorton Gould, theater producer-director Harold Prince, and folk singer-songwriterPete Seeger, in Washington D.C.
Marike de Klerk, ex-wife of former President F.W. de Klerk is murdered at her home in Cape Town
U.S. debut of the first part of two-part TV biopic miniseries "PopeJohn Paul II" on CBS
Ballon d'Or: Milan's Brazilian midfielderKakáis named best football player in the world; first year in which players from clubs outside the UEFA federation eligible for nomination
Pedro Martínezofficially announces his retirement from professional baseball
Miranda Hartwins Popular Non-Fiction Book of the Year for "Is It Just Me?" at The British Book Awards
NBC airs musical "Peter Pan Live!" starring Allison Williams andChristopher Walken; substantially revised from the earlierMary Martinproductions
New Zealand Prime MinisterJohn Keyresigns after 8 years in office
US PresidentDonald Trumpscales back Utah National Parks - Bears Ears National Monument (85%), Grand Staircase-Escalante National Monument (50%)
French PresidentEmmanuel Macrondrops controversial rise in fuel tax after three weeks of mass protests
Theresa May's UK government suffers three parliamentary defeats in one day, also found in contempt of parliament for failing to publish report in full on Brexit
"A Beautiful Noise, TheNeil DiamondMusical" opens at Broadhurst Theatre, NYC
Kennedy Center Honors ceremony held for singers Gladys Knight and Amy Grant, actorGeorge Clooney, rock bandU2, and composer Tania León, in Washington D.C.
Caitlin Clarkposts 22 points, 10 rebounds, and 10 assists for Iowa against Wisconsin, surpassing the Big Ten career triple-double record
Thirty-eight colonists from Berkeley Parish, England, disembark in Virginia and give thanks to God, considered by many the first Thanksgiving in the Americas
Britain's "The Observer" is first published, the oldest Sunday newspaper in the world
Jean-Bédel Bokassacrowns himself Emperor of the Central African Empire in a lavish ceremony costing US$20 million - one third of the nation's budget
Heinrich Meibom, German historian and poet, born in Barntrup, Germany (d. 1625)
Samuel Argall, English adventurer and naval officer, born in East Sutton, Kent, England (d. 1626)
John Cotton, Puritan clergyman in Mass Bay colony, born in Derby, Derbyshire, Kingdom of England (d. 1652)
Jean Chapelain, French writer and poet (La Pucelle), born in Paris (d. 1674)
Alain Emmanuel de Coëtlogon, Marshal of France, born in Rennes, Brittany, France (d. 1730)
Andre Campra, French composer, baptized in Aix-en-Provence, France (d. 1744)
Michel Pignolet de Monteclair, French composer, born in Andelot, Haute-Marne, France (d. 1737)
John Aislabie, English politician (Chancellor of the Exchequer), born in England (d. 1742)
Barbara of Portugal [Maria Madalena Bárbara Xavier Leonor Teresa Antónia Josefa], Queen of Spain, born in Ribeira Palace, Lisbon, Portugal (d. 1758)
Gasparo Gozzi, Italian critic and dramatist, born in Venice (d. 1786)
Jean-Jacques Robson, Flemish composer, born in Dendermonde, Belgium (d. 1785)
William Alexander, Lord Stirling, American general (American Revolutionary War leader), born in New York City (d. 1783)
Henri Grégoire, French Catholic priest and revolutionary leader, coined the term 'vandalism', born in Vého, France (d. 1831)
Juliette Récamier, French socialite, born in Lyon, Kingdom of France (d. 1849)
Johann Gottlob Töpfer, German organist, composer, and teacher, born in Niederroßla, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1870)
Jules Armand Dufaure, French statesman (Prime Minister of France 1871-73), born in Saujon, France (d. 1881)
Emil Aarestrup, Danish physician and poet (Ritornellen), born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1831)
Augustus diZerega, American ship owner (Red Z Line), born in Martinique (d. 1888)
Ernest Panckoucke, French publisher (Horatius), born in Paris (d. 1886)
Elias Smith Dennis, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Newburgh, New York (d. 1894)
William Wing "Old Blizzards" Loring, American Major General (Confederate Army), born in Wilmington, North Carolina (d. 1886)
Hynek Vojáček, Czech composer, born in Zlín, Empire of Austria (d. 1916)
Samuel Butler, English author (Erewhom, Way of All Flesh), born in Langar, Nottinghamshire, England (d. 1902)
Melesio Morales, Mexican composer (Ildegonda; Anita; Sinfonía Vapor), conductor, and teacher, born in Mexico City (d. 1908)
Franz Xavier Wernz, German Superior General of the Society of Jesus, born in Rottweil, Württemberg (d. 1914)
Orest Khvolson, Russian physicist and honorary member of the Soviet Academy of Sciences (1920), born in Saint Petersburg (d. 1934)
William Gunn, English cricket batsman (11 Tests, 1 x 100, HS 102no; Nottinghamshire CCC) and soccer winger (2 caps; Notts County FC), born in Nottingham, England (d. 1921)
Lillian Russell [Helen Leonard], American singer and actress (Great Mogul), born in Clinton, Iowa (d. 1922)
Luther Gulick, American Basketball Hall of Fame contributor (basketball pioneer; international official), born in Honolulu, Hawaiian Islands (d. 1918)
Wassily Kandinsky, Russian-French abstract painter (Dreamy Inspiration), born in Moscow (d. 1944)

Jesse Burkett, American Baseball HOF left fielder (NL batting champion 1895-96, 1901; 240 hits in 1896 MLB record for 15 years; Cleveland Spiders, St. Louis Perfectos/Cardinals), born in Wheeling, West Virginia (d. 1953)
Richard Nicolaas Roland Holst, Dutch artist and painter, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1938)
Dimitur Poljanov [Popov], Bulgarian poet (Iron Verses), born in Karnobat, Bulgaria (d. 1953)
Hamilton Harty, Irish composer and conductor called "the prince of accompanists", born in Hillsborough, Ireland (d. 1941)
Garfield Wood, American inventor, motorboat builder and racer (world water speed record x 5; first to travel over 100 mph on water), born in Mapleton, Iowa (d. 1971)
Erwin von Witzleben, German fieldmarshal (July 20th plot), born in Wrocław, Poland (d. 1944)
Katharine Susannah Prichard, Australian novelist (Black Opal), born in Levuka, Fiji (d. 1969)
Isabel Randolph, American actress (Fuller Brush Girl, Our Miss Brooks), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1973)
Lloyd Bacon, American stage, silent and sound screen actor (Charlie Chaplin films), and director (42nd Street; Knute Rockne, All American), born in San Jose, California (d. 1955)
Bob Shawkey, American baseball pitcher (World Series 1923, 27; AL ERA leader 1920, New York Yankees), born in Sigel, Pennsylvania (d. 1980)
Jack Darragh, Canadian Hockey Hall of Fame right wing (Stanley Cup 1911, 20-21, 23; Ottawa Senators), born in Ottawa, Ontario (d. 1924)
Fritiof Nilsson Piraten, Swedish writer (Bombi Bitt Och Jag), born in Vollsjö, Sweden (d. 1972)
Fung Yu-lan, Chinese philosopher, born in Tanghe County, Henan, Qing Dynasty (d. 1990)
Larry Stock, American songwriter ("Blueberry Hill"; "You're Nobody till Somebody Loves You"), born in New York City (d. 1984)
Nikolai Tichonov, Russian writer (against Pasternak), born in St. Petersburg, Russian Empire (d. 1979)
Mari Andriessen, Dutch sculptor (Dock Worker), born in Haarlem, Netherlands (d. 1979)
Charlie Spencer, English soccer defender and manager (2 caps; Newcastle United; mgr Grimsby Town 1937-51), born in Washington, England (d. 1953)
Charlotte Wynters, American stage and screen actress, born in Wheeling, West Virginia (d. 1991)
Douwe Hermans Kiestra, Frisian farmer and writer, born in Poppenwier, Netherlands (d. 1970)
Adam Walsh, American College Football Hall of Fame center and coach (Notre Dame; Bowdoin), born in Churchville, Iowa (d. 1985)
A. L. Rowse, British author and historian, born in Tregonissey, St Austell, Cornwall (d. 1997)
Al Leader, Canadian-American Hockey Hall of Fame player, referee and administrator (Western Hockey League), born in Barnsley, Manitoba (d. 1982)
Cornell Woolrich, American writer (El Angel Negro), born in New York City (d. 1968)
Siro Cisilino, Italian composer and musicologist, born in Mereto di Tomba, Italy (d. 1987)
Emílio Garrastazu Médici, 28th President of Brazil (1969-74), born in Bagé, Rio Grande do Sul, Brazil (d. 1985)
Jo Boer, Dutch painter and author (Catharina & the Magnolias), born in Surabaya, Indonesia (d. 1993)
Alfred Hershey, American biologist, worked with bacteriophages (Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, 1969), born in Owosso, Michigan (d. 1997)[1]
Jimmy Jewel [James Jewel Marsh], British comedic actor (The Avengers, Thicker Than Water), born in Sheffield, England (d. 1995)
Alex North [Isadore Soifer], American composer (Spartacus; Viva Zapata), born in Chester, Pennsylvania (d. 1991)[1]
Amar Singh, Indian cricket all-rounder (7 Tests, 28 wickets; India's first half-century in Test cricket), born in Rajkot, Gujarat, India (d. 1940)
R. [Ramaswamy] Venkataraman, Indian politician, eighth President of India (1987-92), born in Rajamadam, British India (d. 2009)
Ian Wallace [John Wallace Pritchard], American sci-fi author (Megalomania), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1998)
Robert Adler, Austrian-American inventor (remote control for television), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 2007)
Claude Renoir, French cinematographer (Spy Who Loved Me), born in Paris, France (d. 1993)
Rudolf Hausner, Austrian artist and psychoanalytical painter, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1995)
Allan Jackson, American newscaster (Youth Takes a Stand), born in Hot Springs, Arkansas (d. 1976)
Eddie Heywood, American jazz pianist and composer ("Canadian Sunset", "Soft Summer Breeze"), born in Atlanta, Georgia (d. 1989)
Ely Jacques Kahn, Jr., American writer, born in New York City (d. 1994)
Lew Jenkins, American boxer (World Lightweight Champion 1940-41), born in Milburn, Texas (d. 1981)
Don "Pixie" Roberts, Australian jazz clarinetist, saxophone player, and record label executive (Swaggle), born in Melbourne, Australia (d. 1992)
Frank Kovacs, American tennis player (US pro 1951; US National C'ship 1941 runner-up), born in Oakland, California (d. 1990)
Frank van Klingeren, Dutch architect (De Meerpaal, Dronten; Agora, Lelystad; 't Karregat, Eindhoven), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1999)
I. K. [Inder Kumar] Gujral, Indian politician, Prime Minister of India (1997-98), born in Jhelum, British India (d. 2012)
Jeanne Manford, American gay rights activist, born in Flushing, New York (d. 2013)
Michael Bates, English actor (Clockwork Orange, Patton), born in Jhansi, British India (d. 1978)
Nadir Afonso, Portuguese geometric abstractionist painter, born in Chaves, Portugal (d. 2013)
Ray Robinson, American magazine editor and biographer (Lou Gehrig), born in Manhattan, New York (d. 2017)
(Edna Mae) "Deanna" Durbin, Canadian actress and vocalist (Every Sunday; Three Smart Girls; 100 Men & a Girl), born in Winnipeg, Manitoba (d. 2013)
Gérard Philipe [Philip], French stage and screen actor (Caligula; Le diable au corps (Devil in the Flesh), born in Cannes, France (d. 1959)
Charles Keating, American district attorney (Los Angeles California), born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 2014)
Eagle Keys, American Canadian Football Hall of Fame linebacker and coach (Grey Cup champion 1949, 54-56, 66; Edmonton Eskimos, Saskatchewan R'riders), born in Tompkinsville, Kentucky (d. 2012)
John Krish, British documentary filmmaker (Let My People Go), born in London (d. 2016)
John C. Portman Jr., American architect (Ivan Allen Award-1964), born in Walhalla, South Carolina (d. 2017)
Albert Bandura, Canadian-American psychologist (social learning theory, Bobo doll experiment), born in Alberta, Canada (d. 2021)
George Colerick, English music writer and publisher (Romanticism and Melody; From The Italian Girl to Cabaret), born in London, England (d. 2020)
Maurice Binder, American photographer and film title designer (James Bond Movies), born in New York City (d. 1991)
John Jeffreys, Welsh-English classical composer, and pianist, born in Isle of Thanet, England (d. 2010)
William Labov, American linguist, researcher (sociolinguistics), and educator (University of Pennsylvania, 1976-2015; Columbia, 1964-70), born in Passaic, New Jersey (d. 2024)[1]
Dena Dietrich, American actress ("Mother Nature" in Chiffon margarine commercials), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 2020)
